title: I Stopped Prompting and Started Managing
subtitle: 306 sessions taught me that AI fluency isn't a technical skill. It's a management skill. The data proves it.
content:
Everyone's talking about prompt engineering. After 306 sessions and 1,000 hours with Claude Code, my data says the real skill is something else entirely.

## 122 Mistakes. 8 Rejections.

62 wrong approaches. 60 buggy code instances. 122 total friction events across 306 sessions.

But I only rejected Claude's actions **8 times.**

The other 114? "Not that, try this."

Result: 80% of sessions hit their goal. 243 commits in 30 days. The mistake rate is high. The throughput is higher.

## The 75-Second Loop

Median time between Claude's output and my next message: 75 seconds.

Not crafting a better prompt. Reading the output, deciding if it's on track, and redirecting if it's not. That's the core loop. Claude produces, I evaluate, I course-correct. Repeat.

That's not using a tool. That's managing a direct report at machine speed.

Here's what 75 seconds actually looks like — a real feature build from session #289, adding Stripe donations to this site:

:::terminal{title="Session #289 — Stripe Donations (75s loop)"}
$ Add a one-time donation button using Stripe Checkout. Put it on the support page.
Creating app/support/page.tsx with donation tiers...
Adding Stripe checkout API route at app/api/checkout/route.ts...
Installing stripe package...
Done. Three tiers: $5, $15, $50. Support page renders cards with checkout buttons.
$ Good start but drop the tiers. Single amount, user picks. And use the existing site design — look at the homepage layout first.
Reverting tier cards...
Reading app/page.tsx for design patterns...
Rebuilding with single freeform input matching existing card style...
Done. Single input field, matches homepage card component, forwards to Stripe Checkout.
$ The input needs a dollar sign prefix and default to 5. Also add a thank-you return URL.
Adding $ prefix to input, setting default value to 5...
Creating app/support/thanks/page.tsx...
Updating checkout session success_url...
Done. Input shows $5 default, redirects to /support/thanks after payment.
:::

Three redirects. Zero rejections. Each one took about 10 seconds to type. The feature shipped in under 8 minutes.

## The Interruption Instinct

The most valuable skill I developed: **knowing when to interrupt.**

When Claude starts architecting instead of building, I cut it off. When it builds an entire feature around a data format that doesn't exist — yes, this happened — I call for a revert and move on.

I don't write prompts so perfect that Claude never goes off-track. I expect it to go off-track, and I've gotten fast at catching it.

## It's the Same Playbook

What makes a good engineering manager?

- Set direction without micromanaging implementation
- Give autonomy, then check in
- Interrupt early when the approach is wrong
- Tolerate mistakes when the feedback loop is fast
- Course-correct, don't punish

That's exactly what my data shows. I describe outcomes, not implementations. I let Claude run before I intervene. 114 out of 122 mistakes got a redirect, not a rejection.

The skills that make you effective with AI aren't technical. They're management skills applied at machine speed.

## The Shift

If you've ever managed a talented but junior engineer — writes fast, sometimes picks the wrong approach, needs guardrails on architecture but flies on implementation, produces 10x more with clear direction and fast feedback — you already know how to work with AI.

Stop practicing prompts. Start practicing these:

1. **Describe outcomes, not steps.**
2. **Let it run** before over-specifying.
3. **Interrupt in under 2 minutes** when the approach is wrong.
4. **Redirect, don't reject.** "Not that file, this file."
5. **Tolerate the error rate.** Perfect is the enemy of shipped.

The people who thrive with AI won't be the best prompt engineers. They'll be the best managers.
keyTakeaway: The people who thrive with AI tools won't be the best prompt engineers. They'll be the best managers of AI.
